title: The EOFY Cash Squeeze That Catches Profitable Businesses in July
excerpt: June is often your strongest trading month. It is also when a cluster of financial obligations ripens simultaneously. Profitable businesses run short in July not because of poor performance but because of timing.
content:
June is often one of the strongest trading months in the Australian business calendar. The private health extras rush fills appointment books. Clients push through pre-EOFY purchases. Construction sites run extended hours. Most owner-led businesses along the East Coast report solid revenue figures when June closes and the quarter wraps up.

But June 30 does something else at the same time. It marks the moment when a cluster of financial obligations that have been accumulating across the financial year all come due, or nearly due, within the same narrow window. Superannuation contributions, GST settlements, PAYG installments, insurance renewals, and supplier invoices from businesses clearing their own year-end books arrive together in the period between late June and late July.

For many owner-led businesses, the result is a cash squeeze that arrives in early July and feels genuinely surprising given how well the previous month appeared to go. The squeeze is not caused by poor performance. It is caused by timing. Revenue earned in June sits in debtors until mid-July when payment terms are thirty days or longer. Obligations due in July do not wait. The gap between those two dates is where the pressure concentrates, and it returns, with notable consistency, every year.

The Obligations That Land Together

Superannuation contributions for the April to June quarter must be received by the superannuation fund by 28 July to satisfy the employer's obligation for that quarter. Payments arriving after that date attract the superannuation guarantee charge, which adds cost and additional lodgement requirements. Because fund processing takes several business days, most employers need the payment to leave their account by the second week of July at the latest.

For businesses lodging a quarterly BAS, the April to June return captures three months of GST and PAYG withholding obligations. That settlement is due in late July. For a business that has traded strongly through the quarter, the GST component alone can represent a material cash event, and it arrives at precisely the same moment as the superannuation obligation above.

PAYG installments, the mechanism by which the ATO collects income tax progressively through the year rather than as a lump sum at return time, add another layer. For quarterly installment payers, the fourth installment for the financial year falls in the July lodgment window. Where FY2026 has been a strong year, this installment may be meaningfully higher than the quarterly amounts paid through the year.

Beyond the ATO obligations, the June-to-July window carries its own commercial weight. Annual insurance renewals, subscription and software licences on financial-year billing cycles, and suppliers who are themselves managing year-end cash positions and accelerating collections all arrive in the same period. The obligation stack is almost always deeper than it appears when viewed from any single angle.

Why June Revenue Does Not Automatically Close the Gap

The natural response when a cash pressure looms is to look at how the business is trading. In June, the picture usually looks good. Revenue is up. The order book is full. Confidence is high. The difficulty is that June revenue on standard commercial terms of thirty days does not convert to cash until mid to late July, at the earliest. The business has earned the money. It has not collected it yet.

When the receivables landing window and the obligations due window overlap in July, the business finds itself temporarily short on cash not because it has performed poorly but because two timelines crossed at the same point. This is not a profit problem. The business is profitable. It is a cash flow problem created by the geometry of the financial year, and it is entirely addressable with sufficient lead time.

The businesses that move through EOFY without a July cash crisis are not necessarily larger or better funded. They are the ones who built a forward cash view in May or early June, saw where the gap would fall, and acted before it arrived rather than after.

Building the View Before the Crunch

A 13-week cash flow forecast built in early June does three things that become difficult once the pressure has arrived. It shows when each obligation is actually due, by specific date rather than general category. It shows when debtors are expected to pay, based on their actual payment behaviour rather than their stated terms. And it identifies the specific week or fortnight when the gap between inflows and outflows is at its widest.

With that picture visible, practical responses become available. An accelerated collections effort focused on the highest-value outstanding invoices can bring June cash forward before the obligation window opens. A draw on an existing facility, timed to the specific pressure week and planned in advance, is cheaper and simpler than a reactive drawdown triggered by a falling balance. Discretionary payments that can be deferred past mid-July without operational consequence reduce the size of the gap before it needs to be managed in real time.

A focused working capital review in May or early June surfaces additional levers: which debtors are likely to pay early if asked directly, which supplier payments can be safely positioned toward the back half of July, and what the realistic cash floor looks like before any management action versus after. The difference between those two numbers is the actual breathing room the business has.

ProfitPulse works with East Coast SME owners at exactly this point in the calendar. The EOFY obligation cluster is predictable, the pressure window is consistent from year to year, and the relief comes from visibility built in advance rather than from managing under pressure once July arrives. If you have not yet built a clear picture of your cash position through to the end of July, a conversation now is the right starting point.

faqs.q: Why does cash feel tight in July even when my business traded well in June?
faqs.a: The timing gap is the culprit, not trading performance. Revenue earned in June on thirty-day payment terms typically arrives in mid to late July. Meanwhile, superannuation contributions, the quarterly BAS, and PAYG installments all fall due in the same window. The business has earned the cash. It has not yet collected it when the obligations arrive. This is a timing problem with a predictable shape, and it returns with considerable consistency every year.

faqs.q: What financial obligations does an Australian SME typically face around 30 June?
faqs.a: The most common obligations clustering in the June-to-July window are superannuation contributions for the April to June quarter (due to the fund by 28 July), the quarterly BAS covering GST and PAYG withholding (due late July), and quarterly PAYG installments for income tax. Annual insurance renewals, subscription cycles, and supplier payment terms that synchronise with the financial year compound the picture further for most businesses.

faqs.q: When must Australian employers pay superannuation for the April to June quarter?
faqs.a: Superannuation contributions for the quarter ending 30 June must be received by the employee's fund by 28 July. Payments that miss this deadline attract the superannuation guarantee charge, which adds cost and requires additional lodgement obligations. Because fund processing takes several business days, most employers aim to have the payment leave their account by the second week of July at the latest. A working capital review in May typically surfaces whether cash will be available to meet this date comfortably.

faqs.q: How do I build a cash flow forecast to get through EOFY safely?
faqs.a: Start with a thirteen-week rolling forecast from early June, mapping the specific due dates of each obligation alongside the expected receipt date of each outstanding invoice. Most accounting platforms can export debtor ageing data that anchors the receivables side. The obligations side requires listing each due date explicitly rather than grouping by month. ProfitPulse builds these cash flow models for East Coast SMEs as a standalone engagement or as part of ongoing fractional CFO support.

faqs.q: What should I do if my business will not have enough cash to cover July obligations?
faqs.a: Act in June while options are still available. Accelerate collections on outstanding invoices, beginning with the largest. Review whether any discretionary payments can be deferred to the second half of July without operational cost. If an existing facility is available, plan a draw timed to the specific pressure week rather than waiting for the balance to fall. Speaking to your bank in June is almost always more productive than speaking to them in July.

faqs.q: Does my bookkeeper or accountant handle EOFY cash flow planning for my business?
faqs.a: Your bookkeeper handles the records and lodgements exactly as they should — the BAS, payroll reconciliations, and PAYG summaries that keep the business compliant at year end. Cash flow planning looks forward in time and models the gap between when money is earned and when it arrives; that sits in a different function. Compliance accounting is designed to satisfy the ATO's requirements. Cash flow planning is designed to prevent July from arriving as a surprise.

faqs.q: How does a fractional CFO help with EOFY cash flow planning for an Australian SME?
faqs.a: A fractional CFO arrangement builds the forward cash view that most owner-led businesses lack and installs the rhythm to maintain it month to month. For EOFY specifically, this typically means a thirteen-week forecast built in May, a targeted collections focus through June, and facility planning timed to the obligation window. The EOFY period is one of the highest-value moments for this kind of structured support, because the pressure is predictable and the actions that prevent it are well understood.
